text: Smoke is visibly emitted from various locations along the motor polar forceps during the operation, rather than solely from the tip.
The surgeon uses a robotic endo-wrist stapler to clamp the mesocolon after trimming it, ensuring precise control over the tissue.
The surgeon adjusts the grip to align with the robotic instrument, ensuring precise control during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a robotic endo-wrist stapler to clamp the tissue, following the trimming of the mesocolon.
The surgeon initiates the firing process of the robotic endo-wrist stapler on the clamped section of the mesocolon during the low anterior resection.
The robotic endo-wrist stapler is positioned for firing, but the system alerts the surgical team that the stapler firing has failed.
The surgeon removes the stapler from the surgical field to inspect it after a firing failure alert.
The surgeon inspects the damaged instrument, observing visible signs of wear or malfunction.
Detached pieces of the surgical instrument are visible within the patient's body cavity.
The surgeon locates and removes a fallen piece of the surgical instrument from the patient's body.
The video shows the loosening and disruption of a surgical thread during the procedure.
The video shows a surgical instrument with a visibly loose thread, indicating potential instability or risk of detachment.
The surgeon performs an anterior resection using a single port, focusing on the colorectal area.
The surgeon creates a mesenteric rhythm, manipulating the mesenteric tissue to establish a rhythmic pattern in its movement.
The surgeon attempts to retract the intestines, but the retraction is visibly insufficient.
The surgeon uses one grip to apply traction on the mesentery while employing an endo-wrist stapler to pull through and ligate the inferior mesenteric artery.
After clamping and firing the staplers, the surgeon observes that the ureter has been accidentally transected.
During the dissection, active bleeding is observed in the surgical field.
The surgical team attempts to secure bleeding points and achieve hemostasis in the presacral area, using techniques to control the bleeding observed during dissection.
The surgeon performs rapid and deliberate actions to control unexpected presacral bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon applies packing and compression techniques to manage bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgical team transitions from a minimally invasive approach to open surgery, preparing to address uncontrolled bleeding with direct access to the affected area.
The surgeon slowly rotates the instrument to find the optimal angle for releasing soft tissue from the joint.
The table assistant attempts to pull the soft tissue forcefully, which visually risks causing trauma and bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the rectum appearing extended during the operation, indicating a change in its anatomical positioning.
The surgeon inserts an anal tube into the rectum for decompression, ensuring proper placement to alleviate pressure within the colorectal region.
The surgeon accidentally clamps the anal tube along with the rectum, without first removing the anal tube.
The surgeon fires the stapler, deploying staples to secure the tissue.
The surgeon transects the anal tube, which is visibly separated from the surrounding rectal tissue.
The surgeon performs a lateral dissection to expose the ureter, carefully maneuvering around surrounding tissues to reveal the anatomical structure.
The surgical equipment becomes locked, halting the procedure abruptly.
The surgeon is seated at the computer console near the operating table, controlling the robotic arm during the procedure.
